questions.question: Which ventilator settings best represent lung-protective ventilation for ARDS?
choices: Tidal volume 6 mL/kg predicted body weight (PBW), plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O
Tidal volume 10 mL/kg actual body weight, plateau pressure ≤40 cmH2O
PEEP 0 cmH2O with spontaneous breathing prioritized
FiO2 1.0 continuously to maintain PaO2 ≥150 mmHg
questions.explanation: Lung-protective ventilation in ARDS (ARDSnet trial) uses a tidal volume of 6 mL/kg predicted body weight (not actual body weight) and targets plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O, demonstrating improved mortality. Appropriate PEEP prevents alveolar collapse. Permissive hypercapnia (mild CO2 elevation) is acceptable to achieve low tidal volumes. Prolonged high FiO2 risks oxygen toxicity; target SpO2 88–95% (PaO2 55–80 mmHg) is standard.

questions.question: Select all criteria that represent appropriate indications for ECPR (extracorporeal CPR) in refractory ventricular fibrillation.
choices: Refractory VF persisting after at least 3 defibrillation attempts despite standard ACLS
Suspected reversible cause such as hypothermia or drug toxicity
Reasonable likelihood of survival with good neurological outcome given age and comorbidities
Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) and asystole are absolute contraindications to ECPR
questions.explanation: ECPR criteria vary by institution but generally include: witnessed arrest, refractory VF/VT unresponsive to standard ACLS, presence of reversible causes (hypothermia, drug toxicity, pulmonary embolism), and a reasonable expectation of neurologically intact survival. PEA and asystole are NOT absolute contraindications — certain reversible causes (pulmonary embolism, hypothermia) may still qualify for ECPR. Institutional protocols should guide individual case decisions.
